page.title: List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Yuasa Town)

revision.comment: [[和歌山県]][[有田郡]][[湯浅町]]の[[九十九王子]]旧蹟
revision.text:
'''九十九王子の旧蹟'''（くじゅうくおうじのきゅうせき）では、[[九十九王子|熊野九十九王子社]]の旧蹟のうち、[[和歌山県]][[有田郡]][[湯浅町]]に所在する旧蹟について述べる。
== 九十九王子とは == {{Main|九十九王子}}
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路|紀伊路・中辺路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から[[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[1221年]]（[[承久]]3年）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が湯浅町にある王子を扱う。
== 湯浅町の九十九王子旧蹟 == 湯浅町の九十九王子旧蹟は2社。
=== 逆川王子 === [[九十九王子の旧蹟 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]から糸我峠を越えて下り坂に入り、四辻を過ぎてしばらく進んだ辺りに'''逆川王子'''（さかさがわおうじ）の跡地がある。『熊野道之間愚記』（『[[明月記]]』所収）[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）10月10日条に「逆河王子」とあり、『[[民経記]]』[[承元]]4年（[[1210年]]）4月26日条に「サカサマ王子」という王子の名だけでなく割書きに「水逆流有此有名云々」とある<ref name="Heibon_1997_417">平凡社[1997: 417]</ref>。これは、湯浅湾に流れ込む山田川の支流のひとつで王子の近くを流れる逆川のことで、郡内の他の川が西に向かって流れるのに、この川だけが東に向かって流れることから逆川の名が付き、後に周辺の地名ともなった<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/><ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80">長谷川[2007: 80]</ref>。[[江戸時代]]には吉川村の氏神として崇敬され、境内に地蔵堂があったといい、また、『[[紀伊続風土記]]』はかつて免田5段があったが、[[浅野氏]]に没収されたと記している<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/>。[[1911年]]（[[明治]]44年）に国津神社に合祀されたが、氏子の寄付により1937年（昭和12年）に社殿と石段を新たに建立し、再建された<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80"/>。県指定史跡（[[2009年]]〈[[平成]]21年〉3月17日指定）<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web |author=和歌山県教育委員会 |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=県指定文化財・記念物 |accessdate=2009-04-05 }}</ref>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町吉川925-2
=== 久米崎王子 === 逆川王子から南へ進んで湯浅市街地に入り、紀勢本線の線路を東に越えたところが'''久米崎王子'''（くめさきおうじ）の跡地である。『熊野道之間愚記』建仁元年（1201年）10月10日条に登場し、参詣道より離れた場所にあったため、路傍の木に向かって拝したと記され、『民経記』承元4年（1210年）4月26日条にも「久米崎」なる王子があったと見える<ref>「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会[1985: 399]</ref>。しかし、その後荒廃したものと見られ、[[嘉禎]]2年（[[1236年]]）7月24日付で[[鎌倉幕府]]は湯浅太郎に修復の命令を出しており、そのなかで「紀伊国湯浅庄久米崎王子社、破壊跡無云々」と述べられている（『[[関東御教書]]』）ほか、近世になってからも[[紀州藩]]主[[徳川頼宣]]も跡地に小社を造営して復興につとめた（『紀伊国続風土記』）<ref>平凡社[1997: 260]</ref>。1907年（明治40年）、近隣の顕国神社に合祀された。県指定史跡（[[1959年]]〈昭和34年〉1月8日指定）<ref name="Kinen"/>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町別所322
== 注 == {{Reflist|2}}
== 文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、角川書店（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1） * 長谷川 靖高、2007、『熊野王子巡拝ガイドブック』、新風書房 ISBN 9784882696292 * 平凡社編、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5
== 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Kujuku-ojis|九十九王子}} * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[九十九王子の旧蹟 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]） - '''逆川王子''' - '''久米崎王子''' - （[[九十九王子の旧蹟 (広川町)#井関王子|井関王子]]）
{{Template:九十九王子}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くしゆうくおうしのきゆうせきゆあさ}} [[Category:九十九王子の旧蹟|九十九王子の旧蹟]] [[Category:和歌山県の神社]] [[Category:湯浅町]] [[Category:都道府県指定史跡]]

== 九十九王子とは == {{Main|九十九王子}}
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路|紀伊路・中辺路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から[[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[1221年]]（[[承久]]3年）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が湯浅町にある王子を扱う。
== 湯浅町の九十九王子旧蹟 == 湯浅町の九十九王子旧蹟は2社。
=== 逆川王子 === [[九十九王子の旧蹟 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]から糸我峠を越えて下り坂に入り、四辻を過ぎてしばらく進んだ辺りに'''逆川王子'''（さかさがわおうじ）の跡地がある。「熊野道之間愚記」（『[[明月記]]』所収）[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）10月10日条に「逆河王子」とあり、『[[民経記]]』[[承元]]4年（[[1210年]]）4月26日条に「サカサマ王子」という王子の名だけでなく割書きに「水逆流有此有名云々」とある<ref name="Heibon_1997_417">平凡社[1997: 417]</ref>。これは、湯浅湾に流れ込む山田川の支流のひとつで王子の近くを流れる逆川のことで、郡内の他の川が西に向かって流れるのに、この川だけが東に向かって流れることから逆川の名が付き、後に周辺の地名ともなった<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/><ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80">長谷川[2007: 80]</ref>。[[江戸時代]]には吉川村の氏神として崇敬され、境内に地蔵堂があったといい、また、『[[紀伊続風土記]]』はかつて免田5段があったが、[[浅野氏]]に没収されたと記している<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/>。[[1911年]]（[[明治]]44年）に国津神社に合祀されたが、氏子の寄付により1937年（昭和12年）に社殿と石段を新たに建立し、再建された<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80"/>。県指定史跡（[[2009年]]〈[[平成]]21年〉3月17日指定）<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web |author=和歌山県教育委員会 |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=県指定文化財・記念物 |accessdate=2009-04-05 }}</ref>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町吉川925-2
=== 久米崎王子 === 逆川王子から南へ進んで湯浅市街地に入り、紀勢本線の線路を東に越えたところが'''久米崎王子'''（くめさきおうじ）の跡地である。「熊野道之間愚記」建仁元年（1201年）10月10日条に登場し、参詣道より離れた場所にあったため、路傍の木に向かって拝したと記され、『民経記』承元4年（1210年）4月26日条にも「久米崎」なる王子があったと見える<ref>「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会[1985: 399]</ref>。しかし、その後荒廃したものと見られ、[[嘉禎]]2年（[[1236年]]）7月24日付で[[鎌倉幕府]]は湯浅太郎に修復の命令を出しており、そのなかで「紀伊国湯浅庄久米崎王子社、破壊跡無云々」と述べられている（『[[関東御教書]]』）ほか、近世になってからも[[紀州藩]]主[[徳川頼宣]]も跡地に小社を造営して復興につとめた（『紀伊国続風土記』）<ref>平凡社[1997: 260]</ref>。1907年（明治40年）、近隣の顕国神社に合祀された。県指定史跡（[[1959年]]〈昭和34年〉1月8日指定）<ref name="Kinen"/>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町別所322
== 注 == {{Reflist|2}}
== 文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、角川書店（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1） * 長谷川 靖高、2007、『熊野王子巡拝ガイドブック』、新風書房 ISBN 9784882696292 * 平凡社編、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5
== 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Kujuku-ojis|九十九王子}} * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[九十九王子の旧蹟 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]） - '''逆川王子''' - '''久米崎王子''' - （[[九十九王子の旧蹟 (広川町)#井関王子|井関王子]]）
{{九十九王子}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くしゆうくおうしのきゆうせきゆあさ}} [[Category:九十九王子の旧蹟|九十九王子の旧蹟]] [[Category:湯浅町]] [[Category:都道府県指定史跡]]

revision.text:
本項目では、[[和歌山県]][[有田郡]][[湯浅町]]に所在する'''[[九十九王子]]'''（くじゅうくおうじ）について述べる。
== 九十九王子とは == {{Main|九十九王子}}
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路|紀伊路・中辺路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から [[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[1221年]]（[[承久]]3年）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が湯浅町にある王子を扱う。
== 湯浅町の九十九王子旧蹟 == 湯浅町の九十九王子旧蹟は2社。
=== 逆川王子 === [[九十九王子 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]から糸我峠を越えて下り坂に入り、四辻を過ぎてしばらく進んだ辺りに'''逆川王子'''（さかさがわおうじ）の跡地がある。「熊野道之間愚記」（『[[明月記]]』所収）[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）10月10日条に「逆河王子」とあり、『[[民経記]]』[[承元]]4年（[[1210年]]）4月26日条に「サカサマ王子」という王子の名だけでなく割書きに「水逆流有此有名云々」とある<ref name="Heibon_1997_417">平凡社[1997: 417]</ref>。これは、湯浅湾に流れ込む山田川の支流のひとつで王子の近くを流れる逆川のことで、郡内の他の川が西に向かって流れるのに、この川だけが東に向かって流れることから逆川の名が付き、後に周辺の地名ともなった<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/><ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80">長谷川[2007: 80]</ref>。[[江戸時代]]には吉川村の氏神として崇敬され、境内に地蔵堂があったといい、また、『[[紀伊続風土記]]』はかつて免田5段があったが、[[浅野氏]]に没収されたと記している<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/>。[[1911年]]（[[明治]]44年）に国津神社に合祀されたが、氏子の寄付により 1937年（昭和12年）に社殿と石段を新たに建立し、再建された<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80"/>。県指定史跡（[[2009年]]〈[[平成]]21年〉3月17日指定）<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web |author=和歌山県教育委員会 |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=県指定文化財・記念物 |accessdate=2009-04-05 }}</ref>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町吉川925-2
=== 久米崎王子 === 逆川王子から南へ進んで湯浅市街地に入り、紀勢本線の線路を東に越えたところが'''久米崎王子'''（くめさきおうじ）の跡地である。「熊野道之間愚記」建仁元年（1201年）10月10日条に登場し、参詣道より離れた場所にあったため、路傍の木に向かって拝したと記され、『民経記』承元4年（1210 年）4月26日条にも「久米崎」なる王子があったと見える<ref>「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会[1985: 399]</ref>。しかし、その後荒廃したものと見られ、[[嘉禎]]2年（[[1236年]]）7月24日付で[[鎌倉幕府]]は湯浅太郎に修復の命令を出しており、そのなかで「紀伊国湯浅庄久米崎王子社、破壊跡無云々」と述べられている（『[[関東御教書]]』）ほか、近世になってからも[[紀州藩]]主[[徳川頼宣]]も跡地に小社を造営して復興につとめた（『紀伊国続風土記』）<ref>平凡社[1997: 260]</ref>。1907年（明治40年）、近隣の顕国神社に合祀された。県指定史跡（[[1959年]]〈昭和34年〉1月8日指定）<ref name="Kinen"/>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町別所322
== 注 == {{Reflist|2}}
== 文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、角川書店（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1） * 長谷川 靖高、2007、『熊野王子巡拝ガイドブック』、新風書房 ISBN 9784882696292 * 平凡社編、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5
== 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Kujuku-ojis|九十九王子}} * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[九十九王子 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]） - '''逆川王子''' - '''久米崎王子''' - （[[九十九王子 (広川町)#井関王子|井関王子]]）
{{九十九王子}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くしゆうくおうしゆあさ}} [[Category:九十九王子|ゆあさ]] [[Category:和歌山県の神社]] [[Category:湯浅町]] [[Category:都道府県指定史跡]]

本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が湯浅町にある王子を扱う。
== 湯浅町の九十九王子 == 湯浅町の九十九王子は2社。
=== 逆川王子 === [[九十九王子 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]から糸我峠を越えて下り坂に入り、四辻を過ぎてしばらく進んだ辺りに'''逆川王子'''（さかさがわおうじ）の跡地がある。「熊野道之間愚記」（『[[明月記]]』所収）[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）10月10日条に「逆河王子」とあり、『[[民経記]]』[[承元]]4年（[[1210年]]）4月26日条に「サカサマ王子」という王子の名だけでなく割書きに「水逆流有此有名云々」とある<ref name="Heibon_1997_417">平凡社[1997: 417]</ref>。これは、湯浅湾に流れ込む山田川の支流のひとつで王子の近くを流れる逆川のことで、郡内の他の川が西に向かって流れるのに、この川だけが東に向かって流れることから逆川の名が付き、後に周辺の地名ともなった<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/><ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80">長谷川[2007: 80]</ref>。[[江戸時代]]には吉川村の氏神として崇敬され、境内に地蔵堂があったといい、また、『[[紀伊続風土記]]』はかつて免田5段があったが、[[浅野氏]]に没収されたと記している<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/>。[[1911年]]（[[明治]]44年）に国津神社に合祀されたが、氏子の寄付により 1937年（昭和12年）に社殿と石段を新たに建立し、再建された<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80"/>。県指定史跡（[[2009年]]〈[[平成]]21年〉3月17日指定）<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web |author=和歌山県教育委員会 |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=県指定文化財・記念物 |accessdate=2009-04-05 }}</ref>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町吉川925-2
=== 久米崎王子 === 逆川王子から南へ進んで湯浅市街地に入り、紀勢本線の線路を東に越えたところが'''久米崎王子'''（くめさきおうじ）の跡地である。「熊野道之間愚記」建仁元年（1201年）10月10日条に登場し、参詣道より離れた場所にあったため、路傍の木に向かって拝したと記され、『民経記』承元4年（1210 年）4月26日条にも「久米崎」なる王子があったと見える<ref>「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会[1985: 399]</ref>。しかし、その後荒廃したものと見られ、[[嘉禎]]2年（[[1236年]]）7月24日付で[[鎌倉幕府]]は[[湯浅宗弘|湯浅太郎]]に修復の命令を出しており、そのなかで「紀伊国湯浅庄久米崎王子社、破壊跡無云々」と述べられている（『[[関東御教書]]』）ほか、近世になってからも[[紀州藩]]主[[徳川頼宣]]も跡地に小社を造営して復興につとめた（『紀伊国続風土記』）<ref>平凡社[1997: 260]</ref>。1907年（明治40年）、近隣の顕国神社に合祀された。県指定史跡（[[1959年]]〈昭和34年〉1月8日指定）<ref name="Kinen"/>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町別所322
== 注 == {{Reflist|2}}
== 文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、角川書店（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1） * 長谷川 靖高、2007、『熊野王子巡拝ガイドブック』、新風書房 ISBN 9784882696292 * 平凡社編、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5
== 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Kujuku-ojis|九十九王子}} * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[九十九王子 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]） - '''逆川王子''' - '''久米崎王子''' - （[[九十九王子 (広川町)#井関王子|井関王子]]）
{{九十九王子}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くしゆうくおうしゆあさ}} [[Category:九十九王子|ゆあさ]] [[Category:和歌山県の神社]] [[Category:湯浅町]] [[Category:和歌山県指定史跡]]

revision.comment: /* 関連項目 */
revision.text:
本項目では、[[和歌山県]][[有田郡]][[湯浅町]]に所在する'''[[九十九王子]]'''（くじゅうくおうじ）について述べる。
== 九十九王子とは == {{Main|九十九王子}}
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路|紀伊路・中辺路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から [[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[1221年]]（[[承久]]3年）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が湯浅町にある王子を扱う。

* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町吉川925-2
=== 久米崎王子 === 逆川王子から南へ進んで湯浅市街地に入り、紀勢本線の線路を東に越えたところが'''久米崎王子'''（くめさきおうじ）の跡地である。「熊野道之間愚記」建仁元年（1201年）10月10日条に登場し、参詣道より離れた場所にあったため、路傍の木に向かって拝したと記され、『民経記』承元4年（1210 年）4月26日条にも「久米崎」なる王子があったと見える<ref>「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会[1985: 399]</ref>。しかし、その後荒廃したものと見られ、[[嘉禎]]2年（[[1236年]]）7月24日付で[[鎌倉幕府]]は[[湯浅宗弘|湯浅太郎]]に修復の命令を出しており、そのなかで「紀伊国湯浅庄久米崎王子社、破壊跡無云々」と述べられている（『[[関東御教書]]』）ほか、近世になってからも[[紀州藩]]主[[徳川頼宣]]も跡地に小社を造営して復興につとめた（『紀伊国続風土記』）<ref>平凡社[1997: 260]</ref>。1907年（明治40年）、近隣の顕国神社に合祀された。県指定史跡（[[1959年]]〈昭和34年〉1月8日指定）<ref name="Kinen"/>。

== 注 == {{Reflist|2}}
== 文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、角川書店（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1） * 長谷川 靖高、2007、『熊野王子巡拝ガイドブック』、新風書房 ISBN 9784882696292 * 平凡社編、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5

本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が湯浅町にある王子を扱う。
== 湯浅町の九十九王子 == 湯浅町の九十九王子は2社。
=== 逆川王子 === [[九十九王子 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]から[[糸我峠]]を越えて下り坂に入り、四辻を過ぎてしばらく進んだ辺りに'''逆川王子'''（さかさがわおうじ）の跡地がある。「熊野道之間愚記」（『[[明月記]]』所収）[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）10月10日条に「逆河王子」とあり、『[[民経記]]』[[承元]]4年（[[1210年]]）4月26日条に「サカサマ王子」という王子の名だけでなく割書きに「水逆流有此有名云々」とある<ref name="Heibon_1997_417">平凡社[1997: 417]</ref>。これは、湯浅湾に流れ込む山田川の支流のひとつで王子の近くを流れる逆川のことで、郡内の他の川が西に向かって流れるのに、この川だけが東に向かって流れることから逆川の名が付き、後に周辺の地名ともなった<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/><ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80">長谷川[2007: 80]</ref>。[[江戸時代]]には吉川村の氏神として崇敬され、境内に地蔵堂があったといい、また、『[[紀伊続風土記]]』はかつて免田5段があったが、[[浅野氏]]に没収されたと記している<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/>。[[1911年]]（[[明治]]44年）に国津神社に合祀されたが、氏子の寄付により 1937年（昭和12年）に社殿と石段を新たに建立し、再建された<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80"/>。県指定史跡（[[2009年]]〈[[平成]]21年〉3月17日指定）<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web |author=和歌山県教育委員会 |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=県指定文化財・記念物 |accessdate=2009-04-05 }}</ref>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町吉川925-2
=== 久米崎王子 === 逆川王子から南へ進んで湯浅市街地に入り、紀勢本線の線路を東に越えたところが'''久米崎王子'''（くめさきおうじ）の跡地である。「熊野道之間愚記」建仁元年（1201年）10月10日条に登場し、参詣道より離れた場所にあったため、路傍の木に向かって拝したと記され、『民経記』承元4年（1210 年）4月26日条にも「久米崎」なる王子があったと見える<ref>「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会[1985: 399]</ref>。しかし、その後荒廃したものと見られ、[[嘉禎]]2年（[[1236年]]）7月24日付で[[鎌倉幕府]]は[[湯浅宗弘|湯浅太郎]]に修復の命令を出しており、そのなかで「紀伊国湯浅庄久米崎王子社、破壊跡無云々」と述べられている（『[[関東御教書]]』）ほか、近世になってからも[[紀州藩]]主[[徳川頼宣]]も跡地に小社を造営して復興につとめた（『紀伊国続風土記』）<ref>平凡社[1997: 260]</ref>。1907年（明治40年）、近隣の顕国神社に合祀された。県指定史跡（[[1959年]]〈昭和34年〉1月8日指定）<ref name="Kinen"/>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町別所322
== 注 == {{Reflist|2}}
== 文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、角川書店（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1） * 長谷川 靖高、2007、『熊野王子巡拝ガイドブック』、新風書房 ISBN 9784882696292 * 平凡社編、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5
== 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Kujuku-ojis|九十九王子}} * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[九十九王子 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]） - '''逆川王子''' - '''久米崎王子''' - （[[九十九王子 (広川町)#井関王子|井関王子]]）

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:湯浅町]] to [[Category:湯浅町の建築物]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
本項目では、[[和歌山県]][[有田郡]][[湯浅町]]に所在する'''[[九十九王子]]'''（くじゅうくおうじ）について述べる。
== 九十九王子とは == {{Main|九十九王子}}
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路|紀伊路・中辺路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から [[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[1221年]]（[[承久]]3年）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が湯浅町にある王子を扱う。
== 湯浅町の九十九王子 == 湯浅町の九十九王子は2社。
=== 逆川王子 === [[九十九王子 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]から[[糸我峠]]を越えて下り坂に入り、四辻を過ぎてしばらく進んだ辺りに'''逆川王子'''（さかさがわおうじ）の跡地がある。「熊野道之間愚記」（『[[明月記]]』所収）[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）10月10日条に「逆河王子」とあり、『[[民経記]]』[[承元]]4年（[[1210年]]）4月26日条に「サカサマ王子」という王子の名だけでなく割書きに「水逆流有此有名云々」とある<ref name="Heibon_1997_417">平凡社[1997: 417]</ref>。これは、湯浅湾に流れ込む山田川の支流のひとつで王子の近くを流れる逆川のことで、郡内の他の川が西に向かって流れるのに、この川だけが東に向かって流れることから逆川の名が付き、後に周辺の地名ともなった<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/><ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80">長谷川[2007: 80]</ref>。[[江戸時代]]には吉川村の氏神として崇敬され、境内に地蔵堂があったといい、また、『[[紀伊続風土記]]』はかつて免田5段があったが、[[浅野氏]]に没収されたと記している<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/>。[[1911年]]（[[明治]]44年）に国津神社に合祀されたが、氏子の寄付により 1937年（昭和12年）に社殿と石段を新たに建立し、再建された<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80"/>。県指定史跡（[[2009年]]〈[[平成]]21年〉3月17日指定）<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web |author=和歌山県教育委員会 |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=県指定文化財・記念物 |accessdate=2009-04-05 }}</ref>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町吉川925-2
=== 久米崎王子 === 逆川王子から南へ進んで湯浅市街地に入り、紀勢本線の線路を東に越えたところが'''久米崎王子'''（くめさきおうじ）の跡地である。「熊野道之間愚記」建仁元年（1201年）10月10日条に登場し、参詣道より離れた場所にあったため、路傍の木に向かって拝したと記され、『民経記』承元4年（1210 年）4月26日条にも「久米崎」なる王子があったと見える<ref>「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会[1985: 399]</ref>。しかし、その後荒廃したものと見られ、[[嘉禎]]2年（[[1236年]]）7月24日付で[[鎌倉幕府]]は[[湯浅宗弘|湯浅太郎]]に修復の命令を出しており、そのなかで「紀伊国湯浅庄久米崎王子社、破壊跡無云々」と述べられている（『[[関東御教書]]』）ほか、近世になってからも[[紀州藩]]主[[徳川頼宣]]も跡地に小社を造営して復興につとめた（『紀伊国続風土記』）<ref>平凡社[1997: 260]</ref>。1907年（明治40年）、近隣の顕国神社に合祀された。県指定史跡（[[1959年]]〈昭和34年〉1月8日指定）<ref name="Kinen"/>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町別所322
== 注 == {{Reflist|2}}
== 文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、角川書店（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1） * 長谷川 靖高、2007、『熊野王子巡拝ガイドブック』、新風書房 ISBN 9784882696292 * 平凡社編、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5
== 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Kujuku-ojis|九十九王子}} * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[九十九王子 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]） - '''逆川王子''' - '''久米崎王子''' - （[[九十九王子 (広川町)#井関王子|井関王子]]）

本項目では、[[和歌山県]][[有田郡]][[湯浅町]]に所在する'''[[九十九王子]]'''（くじゅうくおうじ）について述べる。

本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が湯浅町にある王子を扱う。
== 湯浅町の九十九王子 == 湯浅町の九十九王子は2社。
=== 逆川王子 === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|逆川王子跡（逆川神社）]] [[九十九王子 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]から[[糸我峠]]を越えて下り坂に入り、四辻を過ぎてしばらく進んだ辺りに'''逆川王子'''（さかさがわおうじ）の跡地がある。「熊野道之間愚記」（『[[明月記]]』所収）[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）10月10日条に「逆河王子」とあり、『[[民経記]]』[[承元]]4年（[[1210年]]）4月26日条に「サカサマ王子」という王子の名だけでなく割書きに「水逆流有此有名云々」とある<ref name="Heibon_1997_417">平凡社[1997: 417]</ref>。これは、湯浅湾に流れ込む山田川の支流のひとつで王子の近くを流れる逆川のことで、郡内の他の川が西に向かって流れるのに、この川だけが東に向かって流れることから逆川の名が付き、後に周辺の地名ともなった<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/><ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80">長谷川[2007: 80]</ref>。[[江戸時代]]には吉川村の氏神として崇敬され、境内に地蔵堂があったといい、また、『[[紀伊続風土記]]』はかつて免田5段があったが、[[浅野氏]]に没収されたと記している<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/>。[[1911年]]（[[明治]]44年）に国津神社に合祀されたが、氏子の寄付により 1937年（昭和12年）に社殿と石段を新たに建立し、再建された<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80"/>。県指定史跡（[[2009年]]〈[[平成]]21年〉3月17日指定）<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|和書 |author=和歌山県教育委員会 |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=県指定文化財・記念物 |accessdate=2009-04-05 }}</ref>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町吉川925-2
=== 久米崎王子 === 逆川王子から南へ進んで湯浅市街地に入り、紀勢本線の線路を東に越えたところが'''久米崎王子'''（くめさきおうじ）の跡地である。「熊野道之間愚記」建仁元年（1201年）10月10日条に登場し、参詣道より離れた場所にあったため、路傍の木に向かって拝したと記され、『民経記』承元4年（1210 年）4月26日条にも「久米崎」なる王子があったと見える<ref>「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会[1985: 399]</ref>。しかし、その後荒廃したものと見られ、[[嘉禎]]2年（[[1236年]]）7月24日付で[[鎌倉幕府]]は[[湯浅宗弘|湯浅太郎]]に修復の命令を出しており、そのなかで「紀伊国湯浅庄久米崎王子社、破壊跡無云々」と述べられている（『[[関東御教書]]』）ほか、近世になってからも[[紀州藩]]主[[徳川頼宣]]も跡地に小社を造営して復興につとめた（『紀伊国続風土記』）<ref>平凡社[1997: 260]</ref>。1907年（明治40年）、近隣の顕国神社に合祀された。県指定史跡（[[1959年]]〈昭和34年〉1月8日指定）<ref name="Kinen"/>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町別所322
== 注 == {{Reflist|2}}
== 文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、角川書店（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1） * 長谷川 靖高、2007、『熊野王子巡拝ガイドブック』、新風書房 ISBN 9784882696292 * 平凡社編、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5
== 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Kujuku-ojis|九十九王子}} * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[九十九王子 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]） - '''逆川王子''' - '''久米崎王子''' - （[[九十九王子 (広川町)#井関王子|井関王子]]）
{{九十九王子}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くしゆうくおうしゆあさ}} [[Category:九十九王子|ゆあさ]] [[Category:和歌山県の神社]] [[Category:湯浅町の建築物]] [[Category:湯浅町の歴史]] [[Category:和歌山県指定史跡]]

revision.comment: /* 久米崎王子 */
revision.text:
本項目では、[[和歌山県]][[有田郡]][[湯浅町]]に所在する'''[[九十九王子]]'''（くじゅうくおうじ）について述べる。
== 九十九王子とは == {{Main|九十九王子}}
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路|紀伊路・中辺路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から [[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[1221年]]（[[承久]]3年）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が湯浅町にある王子を扱う。
== 湯浅町の九十九王子 == 湯浅町の九十九王子は2社。
=== 逆川王子 === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|逆川王子跡（逆川神社）]] [[九十九王子 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]から[[糸我峠]]を越えて下り坂に入り、四辻を過ぎてしばらく進んだ辺りに'''逆川王子'''（さかさがわおうじ）の跡地がある。「熊野道之間愚記」（『[[明月記]]』所収）[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）10月10日条に「逆河王子」とあり、『[[民経記]]』[[承元]]4年（[[1210年]]）4月26日条に「サカサマ王子」という王子の名だけでなく割書きに「水逆流有此有名云々」とある<ref name="Heibon_1997_417">平凡社[1997: 417]</ref>。これは、湯浅湾に流れ込む山田川の支流のひとつで王子の近くを流れる逆川のことで、郡内の他の川が西に向かって流れるのに、この川だけが東に向かって流れることから逆川の名が付き、後に周辺の地名ともなった<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/><ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80">長谷川[2007: 80]</ref>。[[江戸時代]]には吉川村の氏神として崇敬され、境内に地蔵堂があったといい、また、『[[紀伊続風土記]]』はかつて免田5段があったが、[[浅野氏]]に没収されたと記している<ref name="Heibon_1997_417"/>。[[1911年]]（[[明治]]44年）に国津神社に合祀されたが、氏子の寄付により 1937年（昭和12年）に社殿と石段を新たに建立し、再建された<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_80"/>。県指定史跡（[[2009年]]〈[[平成]]21年〉3月17日指定）<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|和書 |author=和歌山県教育委員会 |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=県指定文化財・記念物 |accessdate=2009-04-05 }}</ref>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町吉川925-2
=== 久米崎王子 === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|2oopx|久米崎王子跡]] 逆川王子から南へ進んで湯浅市街地に入り、紀勢本線の線路を東に越えたところが'''久米崎王子'''（くめさきおうじ）の跡地である。「熊野道之間愚記」建仁元年（1201年）10月10日条に登場し、参詣道より離れた場所にあったため、路傍の木に向かって拝したと記され、『民経記』承元4年（1210 年）4月26日条にも「久米崎」なる王子があったと見える<ref>「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会[1985: 399]</ref>。しかし、その後荒廃したものと見られ、[[嘉禎]]2年（[[1236年]]）7月24日付で[[鎌倉幕府]]は[[湯浅宗弘|湯浅太郎]]に修復の命令を出しており、そのなかで「紀伊国湯浅庄久米崎王子社、破壊跡無云々」と述べられている（『[[関東御教書]]』）ほか、近世になってからも[[紀州藩]]主[[徳川頼宣]]も跡地に小社を造営して復興につとめた（『紀伊国続風土記』）<ref>平凡社[1997: 260]</ref>。1907年（明治40年）、近隣の顕国神社に合祀された。県指定史跡（[[1959年]]〈昭和34年〉1月8日指定）<ref name="Kinen"/>。
* 所在地 和歌山県有田郡湯浅町別所322
== 注 == {{Reflist|2}}
== 文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、角川書店（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1） * 長谷川 靖高、2007、『熊野王子巡拝ガイドブック』、新風書房 ISBN 9784882696292 * 平凡社編、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5
== 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Kujuku-ojis|九十九王子}} * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[九十九王子 (有田市)#糸我王子|糸我王子]]） - '''逆川王子''' - '''久米崎王子''' - （[[九十九王子 (広川町)#井関王子|井関王子]]）

revision.comment: Adding an intro to all of them. It has a magic word instead of a substitution because all of them are gonna be moved in the future to proper pagenames. See comment for more info.
revision.text:
''{{PAGENAME}}'' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group '''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview==

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 九十九王子 (湯浅町). <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview==

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview==

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] to [[Category:qq]]{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}[[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview==

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:new pages]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview==

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:new pages]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview==

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:new pages]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview==

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:new pages]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview==

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview==

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview==

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview==

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview==

This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. revision.comment: trimming off the template
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview==

revision.comment: /* Overview */
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Yuasa Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains **two Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the **Itoga Pass**, descends, and continues past **Yotsutsuji**. The site of **Sakazagawa Ōji** (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions **Sakagawa Ōji** on **October 10, 1201** (*Ken'nin 1*), and the **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as **"Sakasama Ōji"**, adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to **Sakazagawa River**, a tributary of the **Yamada River** that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into **Yuasa Bay**, this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the **Edo period**, it was revered as the **guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village**, and a **Jizō Hall** was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had **five tan of tax-free land**, but this was confiscated by the **Asano clan** {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In **1911** (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into **Kunitsu Shrine**, but in **1937** (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the **shrine building and stone steps** with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **2009** (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds **southward** into the **urban area of Yuasa**, crossing the **Kisei Main Line** railway eastward. This is the location of **Kumezaki Ōji** (久米崎王子).
The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **1201**, *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that **pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside** because the shrine was **located off the main route**. The **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named **"Kumezaki."** {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have **fallen into ruin** at some point, as a **Kamakura shogunate order** dated **July 24, 1236** (*Katei 2*) instructed **Yuasa Tarō** to **repair the shrine**. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the **Edo period**, the **Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu** also rebuilt a **small shrine** at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Ken-koku Shrine**. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **1959** (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Yuasa Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains **two Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the **Itoga Pass**, descends, and continues past **Yotsutsuji**. The site of **Sakazagawa Ōji** (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions **Sakagawa Ōji** on **October 10, 1201** (*Ken'nin 1*), and the **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as **"Sakasama Ōji"**, adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to **Sakazagawa River**, a tributary of the **Yamada River** that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into **Yuasa Bay**, this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the **Edo period**, it was revered as the **guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village**, and a **Jizō Hall** was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had **five tan of tax-free land**, but this was confiscated by the **Asano clan** {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In **1911** (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into **Kunitsu Shrine**, but in **1937** (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the **shrine building and stone steps** with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **2009** (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds **southward** into the **urban area of Yuasa**, crossing the **Kisei Main Line** railway eastward. This is the location of **Kumezaki Ōji** (久米崎王子).
The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **1201**, *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that **pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside** because the shrine was **located off the main route**. The **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named **"Kumezaki."** {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have **fallen into ruin** at some point, as a **Kamakura shogunate order** dated **July 24, 1236** (*Katei 2*) instructed **Yuasa Tarō** to **repair the shrine**. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the **Edo period**, the **Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu** also rebuilt a **small shrine** at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Ken-koku Shrine**. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **1959** (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Yuasa Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains **two Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the **Itoga Pass**, descends, and continues past **Yotsutsuji**. The site of **Sakazagawa Ōji** (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions **Sakagawa Ōji** on **October 10, 1201** (*Ken'nin 1*), and the **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as **"Sakasama Ōji"**, adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to **Sakazagawa River**, a tributary of the **Yamada River** that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into **Yuasa Bay**, this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the **Edo period**, it was revered as the **guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village**, and a **Jizō Hall** was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had **five tan of tax-free land**, but this was confiscated by the **Asano clan** {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In **1911** (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into **Kunitsu Shrine**, but in **1937** (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the **shrine building and stone steps** with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **2009** (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds **southward** into the **urban area of Yuasa**, crossing the **Kisei Main Line** railway eastward. This is the location of **Kumezaki Ōji** (久米崎王子).
The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **1201**, *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that **pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside** because the shrine was **located off the main route**. The **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named **"Kumezaki."** {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have **fallen into ruin** at some point, as a **Kamakura shogunate order** dated **July 24, 1236** (*Katei 2*) instructed **Yuasa Tarō** to **repair the shrine**. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the **Edo period**, the **Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu** also rebuilt a **small shrine** at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Ken-koku Shrine**. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **1959** (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Yuasa Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains **two Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the **Itoga Pass**, descends, and continues past **Yotsutsuji**. The site of **Sakazagawa Ōji** (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions **Sakagawa Ōji** on **October 10, 1201** (*Ken'nin 1*), and the **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as **"Sakasama Ōji"**, adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to **Sakazagawa River**, a tributary of the **Yamada River** that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into **Yuasa Bay**, this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the **Edo period**, it was revered as the **guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village**, and a **Jizō Hall** was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had **five tan of tax-free land**, but this was confiscated by the **Asano clan** {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In **1911** (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into **Kunitsu Shrine**, but in **1937** (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the **shrine building and stone steps** with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **2009** (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds **southward** into the **urban area of Yuasa**, crossing the **Kisei Main Line** railway eastward. This is the location of **Kumezaki Ōji** (久米崎王子).
The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **1201**, *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that **pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside** because the shrine was **located off the main route**. The **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named **"Kumezaki."** {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have **fallen into ruin** at some point, as a **Kamakura shogunate order** dated **July 24, 1236** (*Katei 2*) instructed **Yuasa Tarō** to **repair the shrine**. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the **Edo period**, the **Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu** also rebuilt a **small shrine** at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Ken-koku Shrine**. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **1959** (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Yuasa Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains **two Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the **Itoga Pass**, descends, and continues past **Yotsutsuji**. The site of **Sakazagawa Ōji** (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions **Sakagawa Ōji** on **October 10, 1201** (*Ken'nin 1*), and the **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as **"Sakasama Ōji"**, adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to **Sakazagawa River**, a tributary of the **Yamada River** that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into **Yuasa Bay**, this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the **Edo period**, it was revered as the **guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village**, and a **Jizō Hall** was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had **five tan of tax-free land**, but this was confiscated by the **Asano clan** {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In **1911** (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into **Kunitsu Shrine**, but in **1937** (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the **shrine building and stone steps** with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **2009** (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds **southward** into the **urban area of Yuasa**, crossing the **Kisei Main Line** railway eastward. This is the location of **Kumezaki Ōji** (久米崎王子).
The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **1201**, *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that **pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside** because the shrine was **located off the main route**. The **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named **"Kumezaki."** {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have **fallen into ruin** at some point, as a **Kamakura shogunate order** dated **July 24, 1236** (*Katei 2*) instructed **Yuasa Tarō** to **repair the shrine**. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the **Edo period**, the **Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu** also rebuilt a **small shrine** at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Ken-koku Shrine**. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **1959** (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Yuasa Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains **two Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the **Itoga Pass**, descends, and continues past **Yotsutsuji**. The site of **Sakazagawa Ōji** (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions **Sakagawa Ōji** on **October 10, 1201** (*Ken'nin 1*), and the **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as **"Sakasama Ōji"**, adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to **Sakazagawa River**, a tributary of the **Yamada River** that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into **Yuasa Bay**, this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the **Edo period**, it was revered as the **guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village**, and a **Jizō Hall** was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had **five tan of tax-free land**, but this was confiscated by the **Asano clan** {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In **1911** (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into **Kunitsu Shrine**, but in **1937** (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the **shrine building and stone steps** with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **2009** (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds **southward** into the **urban area of Yuasa**, crossing the **Kisei Main Line** railway eastward. This is the location of **Kumezaki Ōji** (久米崎王子).
The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **1201**, *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that **pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside** because the shrine was **located off the main route**. The **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named **"Kumezaki."** {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have **fallen into ruin** at some point, as a **Kamakura shogunate order** dated **July 24, 1236** (*Katei 2*) instructed **Yuasa Tarō** to **repair the shrine**. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the **Edo period**, the **Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu** also rebuilt a **small shrine** at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Ken-koku Shrine**. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **1959** (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Yuasa Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains **two Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the **Itoga Pass**, descends, and continues past **Yotsutsuji**. The site of **Sakazagawa Ōji** (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions **Sakagawa Ōji** on **October 10, 1201** (*Ken'nin 1*), and the **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as **"Sakasama Ōji"**, adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to **Sakazagawa River**, a tributary of the **Yamada River** that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into **Yuasa Bay**, this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the **Edo period**, it was revered as the **guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village**, and a **Jizō Hall** was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had **five tan of tax-free land**, but this was confiscated by the **Asano clan** {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In **1911** (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into **Kunitsu Shrine**, but in **1937** (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the **shrine building and stone steps** with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **2009** (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds **southward** into the **urban area of Yuasa**, crossing the **Kisei Main Line** railway eastward. This is the location of **Kumezaki Ōji** (久米崎王子).
The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **1201**, *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that **pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside** because the shrine was **located off the main route**. The **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named **"Kumezaki."** {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have **fallen into ruin** at some point, as a **Kamakura shogunate order** dated **July 24, 1236** (*Katei 2*) instructed **Yuasa Tarō** to **repair the shrine**. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the **Edo period**, the **Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu** also rebuilt a **small shrine** at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Ken-koku Shrine**. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **1959** (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Yuasa Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains **two Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the **Itoga Pass**, descends, and continues past **Yotsutsuji**. The site of **Sakazagawa Ōji** (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions **Sakagawa Ōji** on **October 10, 1201** (*Ken'nin 1*), and the **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as **"Sakasama Ōji"**, adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to **Sakazagawa River**, a tributary of the **Yamada River** that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into **Yuasa Bay**, this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the **Edo period**, it was revered as the **guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village**, and a **Jizō Hall** was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had **five tan of tax-free land**, but this was confiscated by the **Asano clan** {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In **1911** (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into **Kunitsu Shrine**, but in **1937** (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the **shrine building and stone steps** with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **2009** (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds **southward** into the **urban area of Yuasa**, crossing the **Kisei Main Line** railway eastward. This is the location of **Kumezaki Ōji** (久米崎王子).
The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **1201**, *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that **pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside** because the shrine was **located off the main route**. The **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named **"Kumezaki."** {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have **fallen into ruin** at some point, as a **Kamakura shogunate order** dated **July 24, 1236** (*Katei 2*) instructed **Yuasa Tarō** to **repair the shrine**. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the **Edo period**, the **Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu** also rebuilt a **small shrine** at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Ken-koku Shrine**. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **1959** (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Yuasa Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains **two Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the **Itoga Pass**, descends, and continues past **Yotsutsuji**. The site of **Sakazagawa Ōji** (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions **Sakagawa Ōji** on **October 10, 1201** (*Ken'nin 1*), and the **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as **"Sakasama Ōji"**, adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to **Sakazagawa River**, a tributary of the **Yamada River** that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into **Yuasa Bay**, this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the **Edo period**, it was revered as the **guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village**, and a **Jizō Hall** was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had **five tan of tax-free land**, but this was confiscated by the **Asano clan** {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In **1911** (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into **Kunitsu Shrine**, but in **1937** (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the **shrine building and stone steps** with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **2009** (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds **southward** into the **urban area of Yuasa**, crossing the **Kisei Main Line** railway eastward. This is the location of **Kumezaki Ōji** (久米崎王子).
The **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **1201**, *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that **pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside** because the shrine was **located off the main route**. The **"Minkeiki"** (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named **"Kumezaki."** {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have **fallen into ruin** at some point, as a **Kamakura shogunate order** dated **July 24, 1236** (*Katei 2*) instructed **Yuasa Tarō** to **repair the shrine**. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the **Edo period**, the **Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu** also rebuilt a **small shrine** at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Ken-koku Shrine**. It was designated a **Prefectural Historic Site** in **1959** (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* **Location**: {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}
