revision.comment: Removing this excess
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)]] to [[List of Kujūku Ōji (Yuasa Town)]]: Text replacement - "List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)" to "List of Kujūku Ōji (Yuasa Town)"
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 湯浅町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Yuasa Town)|ja|湯浅町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Yuasa Town|ja|湯浅町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Yuasa Town|ja|湯浅町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] to [[Category:Completion tier 1]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Yuasa Town|ja|湯浅町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Yuasa Town|ja|湯浅町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

[[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:湯浅町)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:湯浅町)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:湯浅町)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:湯浅町)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:湯浅町)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:湯浅町)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:湯浅町)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] ]] [[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]][[zh:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[ja:湯浅町)]]

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Yuasa Town|ja|湯浅町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Yuasa Town|ja|湯浅町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Yuasa Town|ja|湯浅町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Yuasa Town|ja|湯浅町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
[[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]]
[[Category:qq-湯浅町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}
