<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Yuasa|WD=Q1345625|1=Yuasa, Wakayama}}}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Yuasa, Wakayama|en|Yuasa, Wakayama|WD=Q1345625|lt=Yuasa Town|1=Yuasa, Wakayama}}
==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{Main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Ninety-Nine Ōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Yuasa|WD=Q1345625|1=Yuasa, Wakayama}}}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Yuasa, Wakayama|en|Yuasa, Wakayama|WD=Q1345625|lt=Yuasa Town|1=Yuasa, Wakayama}}
==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{Main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Ninety-Nine Ōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Yuasa|WD=Q1345625|1=Yuasa, Wakayama}}}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Yuasa, Wakayama|en|Yuasa, Wakayama|WD=Q1345625|lt=Yuasa Town|1=Yuasa, Wakayama}}
==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{Main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Ninety-Nine Ōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Yuasa|WD=Q1345625|1=Yuasa, Wakayama}}}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Yuasa, Wakayama|en|Yuasa, Wakayama|WD=Q1345625|lt=Yuasa Town|1=Yuasa, Wakayama}}
==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{Main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Ninety-Nine Ōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (湯浅町)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Yuasa|qid=Q1345625|1=Yuasa, Wakayama}}}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Yuasa, Wakayama|en|Yuasa, Wakayama|qid=Q1345625|lt=Yuasa Town|1=Yuasa, Wakayama}}
==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Yuasa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{Main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Yuasa Town'''.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Yuasa Town == Yuasa Town contains '''two Ninety-Nine Ōji shrines'''.
=== Sakazagawa Ōji === [[File:逆川王子跡.jpg|thumb|Sakazagawa Ōji site (Sakazagawa Shrine)]] From [[Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Arida City)#Itoga Ōji|Itoga Ōji]], the route crosses the '''Itoga Pass''', descends, and continues past '''Yotsutsuji'''. The site of '''Sakazagawa Ōji''' (逆川王子, also written as "Sakasagawa Ōji") is located here. The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (recorded in *Meigetsuki*) mentions '''Sakagawa Ōji''' on '''October 10, 1201''' (*Ken'nin 1*), and the '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) describes it as '''"Sakasama Ōji"''', adding a note stating *"this place is known for its river flowing in reverse."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}.
This refers to '''Sakazagawa River''', a tributary of the '''Yamada River''' that flows near the shrine. Unlike other rivers in the district that flow westward into '''Yuasa Bay''', this river uniquely flows eastward, hence its name, which later became the area's name as well. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}.
During the '''Edo period''', it was revered as the '''guardian deity of Yoshikawa Village''', and a '''Jizō Hall''' was also located within its precincts. *The Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that the shrine once had '''five tan of tax-free land''', but this was confiscated by the '''Asano clan''' {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=417}}. In '''1911''' (*Meiji 44*), it was merged into '''Kunitsu Shrine''', but in '''1937''' (*Shōwa 12*), local parishioners rebuilt the '''shrine building and stone steps''' with donations, restoring the site. {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=80}}. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''2009''' (*Heisei 21*, March 17). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|author=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=April 5, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|2|58.9|N|135|11|0.4|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Yoshikawa 925-2}}
=== Kumezaki Ōji === [[File:久米崎王子.jpeg|thumb|Kumezaki Ōji site]] From Sakazagawa Ōji, the route proceeds '''southward''' into the '''urban area of Yuasa''', crossing the '''Kisei Main Line''' railway eastward. This is the location of '''Kumezaki Ōji''' (久米崎王子).
The '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''1201''', *Ken'nin 1*, October 10) mentions it, noting that '''pilgrims prayed toward a tree by the roadside''' because the shrine was '''located off the main route'''. The '''"Minkeiki"''' (*Shōgen 4*, 1210) also refers to an Ōji named '''"Kumezaki."''' {{Sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|1985|p=399}}.
The shrine appears to have '''fallen into ruin''' at some point, as a '''Kamakura shogunate order''' dated '''July 24, 1236''' (*Katei 2*) instructed '''Yuasa Tarō''' to '''repair the shrine'''. This order describes the site as *"the remains of Kumezaki Ōji Shrine in Yuasa Manor, Kii Province, which has collapsed and disappeared."* (*Kantō Gokyōsho*). During the '''Edo period''', the '''Kishū Domain Lord Tokugawa Yorinobu''' also rebuilt a '''small shrine''' at the site to restore it (*Kii Zoku Fudoki*). {{Sfn|Heibonsha Regional Data Center|1997|p=260}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Ken-koku Shrine'''. It was designated a '''Prefectural Historic Site''' in '''1959''' (*Shōwa 34*, January 8). <ref name="Kinen" />
* '''Location''': {{Coord|34|1|41.2|N|135|10|59.6|E|scale:10000|display=inline|Wakayama Prefecture, Arida District, Yuasa Town, Bessho 322}}
